emails.subject: Re: Fw: IBJ ISDA MASTER AGREEMENT
emails.body: Sorry, Pressed send too early: John, Have had a conversation with IBJ re the CSA, and they have asked us t= o=20 consider proposing a solution that does not involve a CSA. I propose to mee= t=20 with them again to try and explore why, but I would like to be prepared to= =20 offer them a solution whereby we don't enter into a CSA, and we would limit= =20 the types of trades that we could enter into with IBJ. My feeling is that w= e=20 would be unlikely to enter into any long term transactions (over 2 years) a= t=20 this stage anyway, and would prefer to get the relationship going, as I am= =20 not sure they are going to budge on this issue yet. They appear to be quite= =20 willing to discuss all the other points. We can always press for a=20 renegotiation at a later stage if we do want to enter into longer term deal= s.=20 I also want to keep the loan facility completely separate. What are your thoughts on this, and what limitations would you want to put on=20 trades if we do not have a CSA? Thanks, Jonathan =09John Viverito =0909/22/2000 12:04 PM =09=09 =09=09

emails.subject: Re: Fw: IBJ ISDA MASTER AGREEMENT

I propose to mee= t=20 with them again to try and explore why, but I would like to be prepared to= =20 offer them a solution whereby we don't enter into a CSA, and we would limit= =20 the types of trades that we could enter into with IBJ. My feeling is that w= e=20 would be unlikely to enter into any long term transactions (over 2 years) a= t=20 this stage anyway, and would prefer to get the relationship going, as I am= =20 not sure they are going to budge on this issue yet. They appear to be quite= =20 willing to discuss all the other points. We can always press for a=20 renegotiation at a later stage if we do want to enter into longer term deal= s.=20 I also want to keep the loan facility completely separate. What are your thoughts on this, and what limitations would you want to put on=20 trades if we do not have a CSA? Thanks, Jonathan =09John Viverito =0909/22/2000 12:04 PM =09=09 =09=09

emails.subject: Re: Fw: IBJ ISDA MASTER AGREEMENT
emails.body: Jonathan, One thing we might be able to propose is a "Credit Matrix" which prescribes= a=20 Credit Line under which each counterparty can transact with collateral=20 rights and collateral thresholds tied to the credit rating of each=20 counterparty. We have used credit matrixes in several contracts here and= =20 they are appealing in that there are no Material Adverse Change (MAC)=20 clauses. This is appealing to the counterparty in that we cannot=20 automatically drop their collateral threshold to zero when a MAC occurs. W= e=20 are comfortable because as the counterparty's credit quality deteriorates,= =20 the amount of exposure to which we are subject decreases. I would propose to=20 IBJ something like the following in lieu of a full CSA. Jane and John is= =20 it possible to draft these collateral/credit terms into the contract withou= t=20 using a full CSA? (I think every matrix I have used here has been part of a=20 CSA). Rating (S&P) Credit Line AA or above $20MM BBB+ to AA- $15MM BBB $10MM BBB- $7MM BB- to BB+ $5MM Below BB- $1MM These terms are subject to negotiation. Once again, the matrix removes MAC= =20 clauses and the stress counterparties associate with such, but effectively= =20 reduces the potential exposure we have (or THEY have) to a counterparty wit= h=20 diminishing credit quality. What do you all think? John Jonathan Whitehead ENRON 09/25/2000 08:18 PM
